title: Essay on Shiny Live
text: Notes for explaining Shinylive to a coworker, and how it’s different from a normal Shiny app.

title: Essay on Shiny Live
text:
What is Shinylive?
Shinylive is a way to run a Shiny app without a server. Normally Shiny needs R running on a server somewhere. Shinylive instead runs R right inside the person’s web browser using WebAssembly (a thing called webR). So the whole app is just static files - HTML, JS, and the wasm stuff - that you can drop on any plain web host.

title: Essay on Shiny Live
section: How it’s different from a regular Shiny app
text:
How it’s different from a regular Shiny app









Regular Shiny
Shinylive




Where R runs
On a server
In the user’s browser


Hosting
Needs a live R server (shinyapps.io, etc.)
Any static host (GitHub Pages, Netlify, Quarto Pub)


Cost
Can cost money as traffic grows
Basically free to host


Setup
Server has to be maintained
Just static files

title: Essay on Shiny Live
text:
Pros of Shinylive

No server to run or pay for - you can host it for free on something like GitHub Pages.
Nothing to maintain on the back end.
Each user runs their own copy in their browser, so it scales without you doing anything.
Works well embedded inside a Quarto document or website.

title: Essay on Shiny Live
text:
Cons of Shinylive

The first load is slow because the browser has to download R/webR and the packages.
It’s limited to packages that have been built for webR, so not everything works.
The user’s data stays on their machine, which is private but means it can’t talk to a central database.
Heavy computations run on the user’s laptop, so it can be slow for big jobs.

title: Essay on Shiny Live
text:
My take
For a small, shareable app that you just want people to click on, Shinylive is great because it’s free and easy to host. For something bigger that needs a database, heavy computing, or lots of packages, a regular server-based Shiny app is still the better call.

title: Logan Moray
text: Hi, I’m Logan Moray. I’m a Business Marketing student who’s been getting into the data side of marketing, and this website is a quick showcase of what I’ve been learning.

title: Logan Moray
text:
Career aspiration
I want to work in marketing analytics, basically the spot where marketing and data meet. My goal is to help a team make decisions based on what the numbers actually say instead of just guessing, whether that’s digging into campaign performance, building dashboards people can use, or turning messy data into something a stakeholder can act on.

title: Logan Moray
text:
Skills
Stuff I’ve picked up so far:

R and Quarto for wrangling data and building reports
Data visualization with ggplot2
A bit of Python
Google Analytics 4 (certified)
Google BigQuery and Looker Studio (certified)
SQL basics and Excel

title: Logan Moray
text:
Education

Cal Poly Pomona - B.S. in Business Marketing
Coursework in data science with R (IBM 3202), covering data wrangling, visualization, and reproducible reporting with Quarto

title: Essay on Shiny Apps
text: These are my notes for explaining Shiny to a coworker who’s never seen it.

title: Essay on Shiny Apps
text:
What is Shiny?
Shiny is an R package for building interactive web apps straight from R, without having to learn web development. Instead of handing someone a static chart, you can hand them an app where they pick the inputs (a dropdown, a slider, a date range) and the outputs (charts, tables, numbers) update on their own.

title: Essay on Shiny Apps
text:
How it’s built
A Shiny app has two parts:

UI (user interface) - what the person sees: the layout, the input controls, and where the outputs go.
Server - the R code that does the work. It reads the inputs, runs the calculations, and builds the outputs.

The magic word is reactivity. When someone changes an input, Shiny automatically re-runs only the parts of the server that depend on that input and updates the output. You don’t have to write a refresh button or manage any of that yourself.

title: Essay on Shiny Apps
Essay on Shiny Apps
text:
Why it’s useful

Stakeholders can explore the data themselves instead of asking you for a new chart every time.
It’s all in R, so you don’t need a separate front-end language.
Great for dashboards, “what-if” tools, and letting non-coders play with a model.

title: Essay on Shiny Apps
text:
The catch
A regular Shiny app needs a server running R to work (like shinyapps.io or your own server). The R code runs on that server, not in the user’s browser, so someone has to host it and it can cost money as usage grows. That’s the main limitation, and it’s exactly what Shinylive tries to solve.
